evidence.note: The panel works navy by navy through an 80,000-ton replica allowance and names the proposed British, German, Italian, Spanish, French, US, and Russian combinations.

segments.title: A large frigate replacement can justify an Italian domestic class
segments.summary: Italy might buy more Franco-Italian FREMMs, but replacing a large group of older frigates could make a new domestic design economical enough to pursue.
segments.body: The question asks whether the Italian Navy would simply buy more Franco-Italian FREMMs to replace several older frigate groups. The panel says that is possible, but the number of replacement hulls changes the calculation. An order of that scale could be large enough to justify the development cost of a domestic class. The watch point shows how fleet quantity can make a bespoke national design more plausible than extending an existing multinational program.
segments.question: Would the Italian Navy buy more FREMMs to replace its older frigate classes?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, but a replacement program that large could justify developing a new domestic class instead.

evidence.note: The answer allows for more FREMMs but says the large number of replacements could justify the cost of an Italian domestic design.

segments.title: Tillman-style nuclear missile cruisers could trigger allied counterbuilding
segments.summary: In the alternate scenario, a strong Soviet counter to Tillman-style nuclear missile cruisers would make it politically harder for Britain and France not to build comparable capabilities.
segments.body: The scenario asks how the Soviet Union and the United States' allies would respond to Tillman-style nuclear missile cruisers. The panel makes the outcome conditional on funding and on how strongly the ships affect international perceptions. If the Soviet Navy begins building counters, Britain and France in particular would face greater pressure to acquire capabilities in the same area. The answer treats an arms race as a political and strategic feedback loop rather than an automatic ship-for-ship response.
segments.question: How would the Soviets react to Tillman-style nuclear missile cruisers, and would US allies build ships in response?
segments.answerShort: It depends on funding and strategic impact, but visible Soviet counterbuilding would make British and French programs much harder to avoid.

evidence.note: The answer ties allied construction to funding, the ships' international impact, and whether the Soviets begin building counters.

segments.title: A fifteen-ship US battle line would rewrite the treaty-era balance
segments.summary: Four Colorados, five 1920 South Dakotas, and six Lexingtons would form a formidable US battle line, but its survival into the Second World War would also transform rival fleets and the treaty system.
segments.body: The proposed US battle line contains four Colorado-class battleships, five ships of the unbuilt 1920 South Dakota class, and six Lexington-class battlecruisers. The panel warns that this force cannot simply be inserted into the historical Second World War while every other navy remains unchanged. A fifteen-ship concentration of roughly 40,000-ton capital ships would have produced a very different treaty regime and prompted Britain, Japan, and other powers to retain or build different battle lines. Its wartime value would also depend on how thoroughly the ships were modernized during peacetime. Even with those qualifications, the panel calls it a very capable and intimidating force with a large concentration of 16-inch guns.
segments.question: How capable would a US Second World War battle line of four Colorados, five 1920 South Dakotas, and six Lexingtons be?
segments.answerShort: Extremely capable, but its existence would also change naval treaties, rival building programs, and the modernization history leading into the war.

evidence.note: The panel counts the fifteen ships, emphasizes their 16-inch armament, and explains that rival fleets, treaties, and peacetime upgrades would all differ.

segments.title: A Second World War sloop turns Trafalgar into a standoff action
segments.summary: A Second World War sloop at Trafalgar could ask Nelson to lead a line or stand off and use its longer-ranged 4-inch high-explosive guns against the Franco-Spanish fleet.
segments.body: The time-travel scenario places a Second World War-era sloop with Nelson immediately before Trafalgar. Its commander would explain the ship's capabilities and either request the honor of leading a battle line or operate independently at standoff range. The panel argues that its 4-inch high-explosive guns would outrange the sailing fleet's weapons and assumes that a single modern shell could badly damage each wooden warship. A Black Swan-class sloop with twin 4-inch mounts would make the imbalance still greater. The counterfactual turns Trafalgar into a concise illustration of the range and explosive-effect gap between industrial-era naval gunnery and wooden sailing warships.
segments.question: How should a Second World War-era sloop arriving before Trafalgar persuade Nelson to use it, and what role should it take?
segments.answerShort: Offer to lead a line or stand off independently and use the sloop's longer-ranged 4-inch high-explosive guns against the Franco-Spanish fleet.

evidence.note: The answer proposes leading a line or firing from standoff range, emphasizes 4-inch high-explosive shells, and notes that a Black Swan's twin mount would widen the advantage.
